2777 * Block and complete a connection.
2778 *
2779 * Returns 1 on success, 0 on failure.
2780 */
2781int
2782pqConnectDBComplete(PGconn *conn)
2783{
2784	PostgresPollingStatusType flag = PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
2785	pg_usec_time_t end_time = -1;
2786	int			timeout = 0;
2787	int			last_whichhost = -2;	/* certainly different from whichhost */
2788	int			last_whichaddr = -2;	/* certainly different from whichaddr */
2789
2790	if (conn == NULL || conn->status == CONNECTION_BAD)
2791		return 0;
2792
2793	/*
2794	 * Set up a time limit, if connect_timeout is greater than zero.
2795	 */
2796	if (conn->connect_timeout != NULL)
2797	{
2798		if (!pqParseIntParam(conn->connect_timeout, &timeout, conn,
2799							 "connect_timeout"))
2800		{
2801			/* mark the connection as bad to report the parsing failure */
2802			conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
2803			return 0;
2804		}
2805	}
2806
2807	for (;;)
2808	{
2809		int			ret = 0;
2810
2811		/*
2812		 * (Re)start the connect_timeout timer if it's active and we are
2813		 * considering a different host than we were last time through.  If
2814		 * we've already succeeded, though, needn't recalculate.
2815		 */
2816		if (flag != PGRES_POLLING_OK &&
2817			timeout > 0 &&
2818			(conn->whichhost != last_whichhost ||
2819			 conn->whichaddr != last_whichaddr))
2820		{
2821			end_time = PQgetCurrentTimeUSec() + (pg_usec_time_t) timeout * 1000000;
2822			last_whichhost = conn->whichhost;
2823			last_whichaddr = conn->whichaddr;
2824		}
2825
2826		/*
2827		 * Wait, if necessary.  Note that the initial state (just after
2828		 * PQconnectStart) is to wait for the socket to select for writing.
2829		 */
2830		switch (flag)
2831		{
2832			case PGRES_POLLING_OK:
2833				return 1;		/* success! */
2834
2835			case PGRES_POLLING_READING:
2836				ret = pqWaitTimed(1, 0, conn, end_time);
2837				if (ret == -1)
2838				{
2839					/* hard failure, eg select() problem, aborts everything */
2840					conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
2841					return 0;
2842				}
2843				break;
2844
2845			case PGRES_POLLING_WRITING:
2846				ret = pqWaitTimed(0, 1, conn, end_time);
2847				if (ret == -1)
2848				{
2849					/* hard failure, eg select() problem, aborts everything */
2850					conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
2851					return 0;
2852				}
2853				break;
2854
2855			default:
2856				/* Just in case we failed to set it in PQconnectPoll */
2857				conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
2858				return 0;
2859		}
2860
2861		if (ret == 1)			/* connect_timeout elapsed */
2862		{
2863			/*
2864			 * Give up on current server/address, try the next one.
2865			 */
2866			conn->try_next_addr = true;
2867			conn->status = CONNECTION_NEEDED;
2868		}
2869
2870		/*
2871		 * Now try to advance the state machine.
2872		 */
2873		if (conn->cancelRequest)
2874			flag = PQcancelPoll((PGcancelConn *) conn);
2875		else
2876			flag = PQconnectPoll(conn);
2877	}
2878}
2879
2880/* ----------------
2881 *		PQconnectPoll
2882 *
2883 * Poll an asynchronous connection.
2884 *
2885 * Returns a PostgresPollingStatusType.
2886 * Before calling this function, use select(2) to determine when data
2887 * has arrived..
2888 *
2889 * You must call PQfinish whether or not this fails.
2890 *
2891 * This function and PQconnectStart are intended to allow connections to be
2892 * made without blocking the execution of your program on remote I/O. However,
2893 * there are a number of caveats:
2894 *
2895 *	 o	If you call PQtrace, ensure that the stream object into which you trace
2896 *		will not block.
2897 *	 o	If you do not supply an IP address for the remote host (i.e. you
2898 *		supply a host name instead) then PQconnectStart will block on
2899 *		getaddrinfo.  You will be fine if using Unix sockets (i.e. by
2900 *		supplying neither a host name nor a host address).
2901 *	 o	If your backend wants to use Kerberos authentication then you must
2902 *		supply both a host name and a host address, otherwise this function
2903 *		may block on gethostname.
2904 *
2905 * ----------------
2906 */
2907PostgresPollingStatusType
2908PQconnectPoll(PGconn *conn)
2909{
2910	bool		reset_connection_state_machine = false;
2911	bool		need_new_connection = false;
2912	PGresult   *res;
2913	char		sebuf[PG_STRERROR_R_BUFLEN];
2914	int			optval;
2915
2916	if (conn == NULL)
2917		return PGRES_POLLING_FAILED;
2918
2919	/* Get the new data */
2920	switch (conn->status)
2921	{
2922			/*
2923			 * We really shouldn't have been polled in these two cases, but we
2924			 * can handle it.
2925			 */
2926		case CONNECTION_BAD:
2927			return PGRES_POLLING_FAILED;
2928		case CONNECTION_OK:
2929			return PGRES_POLLING_OK;
2930
2931			/* These are reading states */
2932		case CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE:
2933		case CONNECTION_AUTH_OK:
2934		case CONNECTION_CHECK_WRITABLE:
2935		case CONNECTION_CONSUME:
2936		case CONNECTION_CHECK_STANDBY:
2937			{
2938				/* Load waiting data */
2939				int			n = pqReadData(conn);
2940
2941				if (n < 0)
2942					goto error_return;
2943				if (n == 0)
2944					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
2945
2946				break;
2947			}
2948
2949			/* These are writing states, so we just proceed. */
2950		case CONNECTION_STARTED:
2951		case CONNECTION_MADE:
2952			break;
2953
2954			/* Special cases: proceed without waiting. */
2955		case CONNECTION_SSL_STARTUP:
2956		case CONNECTION_NEEDED:
2957		case CONNECTION_GSS_STARTUP:
2958		case CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET:
2959		case CONNECTION_AUTHENTICATING:
2960			break;
2961
2962		default:
2963			libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "invalid connection state, probably indicative of memory corruption");
2964			goto error_return;
2965	}
2966
2967
2968keep_going:						/* We will come back to here until there is
2969								 * nothing left to do. */
2970
2971	/* Time to advance to next address, or next host if no more addresses? */
2972	if (conn->try_next_addr)
2973	{
2974		if (conn->whichaddr < conn->naddr)
2975		{
2976			conn->whichaddr++;
2977			reset_connection_state_machine = true;
2978		}
2979		else
2980			conn->try_next_host = true;
2981		conn->try_next_addr = false;
2982	}
2983
2984	/* Time to advance to next connhost[] entry? */
2985	if (conn->try_next_host)
2986	{
2987		pg_conn_host *ch;
2988		struct addrinfo hint;
2989		struct addrinfo *addrlist;
2990		int			thisport;
2991		int			ret;
2992		char		portstr[MAXPGPATH];
2993
2994		if (conn->whichhost + 1 < conn->nconnhost)
2995			conn->whichhost++;
2996		else
2997		{
2998			/*
2999			 * Oops, no more hosts.
3000			 *
3001			 * If we are trying to connect in "prefer-standby" mode, then drop
3002			 * the standby requirement and start over. Don't do this for
3003			 * cancel requests though, since we are certain the list of
3004			 * servers won't change as the target_server_type option is not
3005			 * applicable to those connections.
3006			 *
3007			 * Otherwise, an appropriate error message is already set up, so
3008			 * we just need to set the right status.
3009			 */
3010			if (conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_PREFER_STANDBY &&
3011				conn->nconnhost > 0 &&
3012				!conn->cancelRequest)
3013			{
3014				conn->target_server_type = SERVER_TYPE_PREFER_STANDBY_PASS2;
3015				conn->whichhost = 0;
3016			}
3017			else
3018				goto error_return;
3019		}
3020
3021		/* Drop any address info for previous host */
3022		release_conn_addrinfo(conn);
3023
3024		/*
3025		 * Look up info for the new host.  On failure, log the problem in
3026		 * conn->errorMessage, then loop around to try the next host.  (Note
3027		 * we don't clear try_next_host until we've succeeded.)
3028		 */
3029		ch = &conn->connhost[conn->whichhost];
3030
3031		/* Initialize hint structure */
3032		MemSet(&hint, 0, sizeof(hint));
3033		hint.ai_socktype = SOCK_STREAM;
3034		hint.ai_family = AF_UNSPEC;
3035
3036		/* Figure out the port number we're going to use. */
3037		if (ch->port == NULL || ch->port[0] == '\0')
3038			thisport = DEF_PGPORT;
3039		else
3040		{
3041			if (!pqParseIntParam(ch->port, &thisport, conn, "port"))
3042				goto error_return;
3043
3044			if (thisport < 1 || thisport > 65535)
3045			{
3046				libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "invalid port number: \"%s\"", ch->port);
3047				goto keep_going;
3048			}
3049		}
3050		snprintf(portstr, sizeof(portstr), "%d", thisport);
3051
3052		/* Use pg_getaddrinfo_all() to resolve the address */
3053		switch (ch->type)
3054		{
3055			case CHT_HOST_NAME:
3056				ret = pg_getaddrinfo_all(ch->host, portstr, &hint,
3057										 &addrlist);
3058				if (ret || !addrlist)
3059				{
3060					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not translate host name \"%s\" to address: %s",
3061											ch->host, gai_strerror(ret));
3062					goto keep_going;
3063				}
3064				break;
3065
3066			case CHT_HOST_ADDRESS:
3067				hint.ai_flags = AI_NUMERICHOST;
3068				ret = pg_getaddrinfo_all(ch->hostaddr, portstr, &hint,
3069										 &addrlist);
3070				if (ret || !addrlist)
3071				{
3072					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not parse network address \"%s\": %s",
3073											ch->hostaddr, gai_strerror(ret));
3074					goto keep_going;
3075				}
3076				break;
3077
3078			case CHT_UNIX_SOCKET:
3079				hint.ai_family = AF_UNIX;
3080				UNIXSOCK_PATH(portstr, thisport, ch->host);
3081				if (strlen(portstr) >= UNIXSOCK_PATH_BUFLEN)
3082				{
3083					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "Unix-domain socket path \"%s\" is too long (maximum %d bytes)",
3084											portstr,
3085											(int) (UNIXSOCK_PATH_BUFLEN - 1));
3086					goto keep_going;
3087				}
3088
3089				/*
3090				 * NULL hostname tells pg_getaddrinfo_all to parse the service
3091				 * name as a Unix-domain socket path.
3092				 */
3093				ret = pg_getaddrinfo_all(NULL, portstr, &hint,
3094										 &addrlist);
3095				if (ret || !addrlist)
3096				{
3097					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not translate Unix-domain socket path \"%s\" to address: %s",
3098											portstr, gai_strerror(ret));
3099					goto keep_going;
3100				}
3101				break;
3102		}
3103
3104		/*
3105		 * Store a copy of the addrlist in private memory so we can perform
3106		 * randomization for load balancing.
3107		 */
3108		ret = store_conn_addrinfo(conn, addrlist);
3109		pg_freeaddrinfo_all(hint.ai_family, addrlist);
3110		if (ret)
3111			goto error_return;	/* message already logged */
3112
3113		/*
3114		 * If random load balancing is enabled we shuffle the addresses.
3115		 */
3116		if (conn->load_balance_type == LOAD_BALANCE_RANDOM)
3117		{
3118			/*
3119			 * This is the "inside-out" variant of the Fisher-Yates shuffle
3120			 * algorithm. Notionally, we append each new value to the array
3121			 * and then swap it with a randomly-chosen array element (possibly
3122			 * including itself, else we fail to generate permutations with
3123			 * the last integer last).  The swap step can be optimized by
3124			 * combining it with the insertion.
3125			 *
3126			 * We don't need to initialize conn->prng_state here, because that
3127			 * already happened in pqConnectOptions2.
3128			 */
3129			for (int i = 1; i < conn->naddr; i++)
3130			{
3131				int			j = pg_prng_uint64_range(&conn->prng_state, 0, i);
3132				AddrInfo	temp = conn->addr[j];
3133
3134				conn->addr[j] = conn->addr[i];
3135				conn->addr[i] = temp;
3136			}
3137		}
3138
3139		reset_connection_state_machine = true;
3140		conn->try_next_host = false;
3141	}
3142
3143	/* Reset connection state machine? */
3144	if (reset_connection_state_machine)
3145	{
3146		/*
3147		 * (Re) initialize our connection control variables for a set of
3148		 * connection attempts to a single server address.  These variables
3149		 * must persist across individual connection attempts, but we must
3150		 * reset them when we start to consider a new server.
3151		 */
3152		conn->pversion = conn->max_pversion;
3153		conn->send_appname = true;
3154		conn->failed_enc_methods = 0;
3155		conn->current_enc_method = 0;
3156		conn->allowed_enc_methods = 0;
3157		reset_connection_state_machine = false;
3158		need_new_connection = true;
3159	}
3160
3161	/* Force a new connection (perhaps to the same server as before)? */
3162	if (need_new_connection)
3163	{
3164		/* Drop any existing connection */
3165		pqDropConnection(conn, true);
3166
3167		/* Reset all state obtained from old server */
3168		pqDropServerData(conn);
3169
3170		/* Drop any PGresult we might have, too */
3171		conn->asyncStatus = PGASYNC_IDLE;
3172		conn->xactStatus = PQTRANS_IDLE;
3173		conn->pipelineStatus = PQ_PIPELINE_OFF;
3174		pqClearAsyncResult(conn);
3175
3176		/* Reset conn->status to put the state machine in the right state */
3177		conn->status = CONNECTION_NEEDED;
3178
3179		need_new_connection = false;
3180	}
3181
3182	/*
3183	 * Decide what to do next, if server rejects SSL or GSS negotiation, but
3184	 * the connection is still valid.  If there are no options left, error out
3185	 * with 'msg'.
3186	 */
3187#define ENCRYPTION_NEGOTIATION_FAILED(msg) \
3188	do { \
3189		switch (encryption_negotiation_failed(conn)) \
3190		{ \
3191			case 0: \
3192				libpq_append_conn_error(conn, (msg)); \
3193				goto error_return; \
3194			case 1: \
3195				conn->status = CONNECTION_MADE; \
3196				return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING; \
3197			case 2: \
3198				need_new_connection = true; \
3199				goto keep_going; \
3200		} \
3201	} while(0);
3202
3203	/*
3204	 * Decide what to do next, if connection fails.  If there are no options
3205	 * left, return with an error.  The error message has already been written
3206	 * to the connection's error buffer.
3207	 */
3208#define CONNECTION_FAILED() \
3209	do { \
3210		if (connection_failed(conn)) \
3211		{ \
3212			need_new_connection = true; \
3213			goto keep_going; \
3214		} \
3215		else \
3216			goto error_return; \
3217	} while(0);
3218
3219	/* Now try to advance the state machine for this connection */
3220	switch (conn->status)
3221	{
3222		case CONNECTION_NEEDED:
3223			{
3224				/*
3225				 * Try to initiate a connection to one of the addresses
3226				 * returned by pg_getaddrinfo_all().  conn->whichaddr is the
3227				 * next one to try.
3228				 *
3229				 * The extra level of braces here is historical.  It's not
3230				 * worth reindenting this whole switch case to remove 'em.
3231				 */
3232				{
3233					char		host_addr[NI_MAXHOST];
3234					int			sock_type;
3235					AddrInfo   *addr_cur;
3236
3237					/*
3238					 * Advance to next possible host, if we've tried all of
3239					 * the addresses for the current host.
3240					 */
3241					if (conn->whichaddr == conn->naddr)
3242					{
3243						conn->try_next_host = true;
3244						goto keep_going;
3245					}
3246					addr_cur = &conn->addr[conn->whichaddr];
3247
3248					/* Remember current address for possible use later */
3249					memcpy(&conn->raddr, &addr_cur->addr, sizeof(SockAddr));
3250
3251#ifdef ENABLE_GSS
3252
3253					/*
3254					 * Before establishing the connection, check if it's
3255					 * doomed to fail because gssencmode='require' but GSSAPI
3256					 * is not available.
3257					 */
3258					if (conn->gssencmode[0] == 'r')
3259					{
3260						if (conn->raddr.addr.ss_family == AF_UNIX)
3261						{
3262							libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
3263													"GSSAPI encryption required but it is not supported over a local socket");
3264							goto error_return;
3265						}
3266						if (conn->gcred == GSS_C_NO_CREDENTIAL)
3267						{
3268							if (!pg_GSS_have_cred_cache(&conn->gcred))
3269							{
3270								libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
3271														"GSSAPI encryption required but no credential cache");
3272								goto error_return;
3273							}
3274						}
3275					}
3276#endif
3277
3278					/*
3279					 * Choose the encryption method to try first.  Do this
3280					 * before establishing the connection, so that if none of
3281					 * the modes allowed by the connections options are
3282					 * available, we can error out before establishing the
3283					 * connection.
3284					 */
3285					if (!init_allowed_encryption_methods(conn))
3286						goto error_return;
3287
3288					/*
3289					 * Set connip, too.  Note we purposely ignore strdup
3290					 * failure; not a big problem if it fails.
3291					 */
3292					if (conn->connip != NULL)
3293					{
3294						free(conn->connip);
3295						conn->connip = NULL;
3296					}
3297					getHostaddr(conn, host_addr, NI_MAXHOST);
3298					if (host_addr[0])
3299						conn->connip = strdup(host_addr);
3300
3301					/* Try to create the socket */
3302					sock_type = SOCK_STREAM;
3303#ifdef SOCK_CLOEXEC
3304
3305					/*
3306					 * Atomically mark close-on-exec, if possible on this
3307					 * platform, so that there isn't a window where a
3308					 * subprogram executed by another thread inherits the
3309					 * socket.  See fallback code below.
3310					 */
3311					sock_type |= SOCK_CLOEXEC;
3312#endif
3313#ifdef SOCK_NONBLOCK
3314
3315					/*
3316					 * We might as well skip a system call for nonblocking
3317					 * mode too, if we can.
3318					 */
3319					sock_type |= SOCK_NONBLOCK;
3320#endif
3321					conn->sock = socket(addr_cur->family, sock_type, 0);
3322					if (conn->sock == PGINVALID_SOCKET)
3323					{
3324						int			errorno = SOCK_ERRNO;
3325
3326						/*
3327						 * Silently ignore socket() failure if we have more
3328						 * addresses to try; this reduces useless chatter in
3329						 * cases where the address list includes both IPv4 and
3330						 * IPv6 but kernel only accepts one family.
3331						 */
3332						if (conn->whichaddr < conn->naddr ||
3333							conn->whichhost + 1 < conn->nconnhost)
3334						{
3335							conn->try_next_addr = true;
3336							goto keep_going;
3337						}
3338						emitHostIdentityInfo(conn, host_addr);
3339						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not create socket: %s",
3340												SOCK_STRERROR(errorno, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3341						goto error_return;
3342					}
3343
3344					/*
3345					 * Once we've identified a target address, all errors
3346					 * except the preceding socket()-failure case should be
3347					 * prefixed with host-identity information.  (If the
3348					 * connection succeeds, the contents of conn->errorMessage
3349					 * won't matter, so this is harmless.)
3350					 */
3351					emitHostIdentityInfo(conn, host_addr);
3352
3353					/*
3354					 * Select socket options: no delay of outgoing data for
3355					 * TCP sockets, nonblock mode, close-on-exec.  Try the
3356					 * next address if any of this fails.
3357					 */
3358					if (addr_cur->family != AF_UNIX)
3359					{
3360						if (!connectNoDelay(conn))
3361						{
3362							/* error message already created */
3363							conn->try_next_addr = true;
3364							goto keep_going;
3365						}
3366					}
3367#ifndef SOCK_NONBLOCK
3368					if (!pg_set_noblock(conn->sock))
3369					{
3370						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not set socket to nonblocking mode: %s",
3371												SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3372						conn->try_next_addr = true;
3373						goto keep_going;
3374					}
3375#endif
3376
3377#ifndef SOCK_CLOEXEC
3378#ifdef F_SETFD
3379					if (fcntl(conn->sock, F_SETFD, FD_CLOEXEC) == -1)
3380					{
3381						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not set socket to close-on-exec mode: %s",
3382												SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3383						conn->try_next_addr = true;
3384						goto keep_going;
3385					}
3386#endif							/* F_SETFD */
3387#endif
3388
3389					if (addr_cur->family != AF_UNIX)
3390					{
3391#ifndef WIN32
3392						int			on = 1;
3393#endif
3394						int			usekeepalives = useKeepalives(conn);
3395						int			err = 0;
3396
3397						if (usekeepalives < 0)
3398						{
3399							/* error is already reported */
3400							err = 1;
3401						}
3402						else if (usekeepalives == 0)
3403						{
3404							/* Do nothing */
3405						}
3406#ifndef WIN32
3407						else if (setsockopt(conn->sock,
3408											SOL_SOCKET, SO_KEEPALIVE,
3409											(char *) &on, sizeof(on)) < 0)
3410						{
3411							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "%s(%s) failed: %s",
3412													"setsockopt",
3413													"SO_KEEPALIVE",
3414													SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3415							err = 1;
3416						}
3417						else if (!setKeepalivesIdle(conn)
3418								 || !setKeepalivesInterval(conn)
3419								 || !setKeepalivesCount(conn))
3420							err = 1;
3421#else							/* WIN32 */
3422#ifdef SIO_KEEPALIVE_VALS
3423						else if (!prepKeepalivesWin32(conn))
3424							err = 1;
3425#endif							/* SIO_KEEPALIVE_VALS */
3426#endif							/* WIN32 */
3427						else if (!setTCPUserTimeout(conn))
3428							err = 1;
3429
3430						if (err)
3431						{
3432							conn->try_next_addr = true;
3433							goto keep_going;
3434						}
3435					}
3436
3437					/*----------
3438					 * We have three methods of blocking SIGPIPE during
3439					 * send() calls to this socket:
3440					 *
3441					 *	- setsockopt(sock, SO_NOSIGPIPE)
3442					 *	- send(sock, ..., MSG_NOSIGNAL)
3443					 *	- setting the signal mask to SIG_IGN during send()
3444					 *
3445					 * The third method requires three syscalls per send,
3446					 * so we prefer either of the first two, but they are
3447					 * less portable.  The state is tracked in the following
3448					 * members of PGconn:
3449					 *
3450					 * conn->sigpipe_so		- we have set up SO_NOSIGPIPE
3451					 * conn->sigpipe_flag	- we're specifying MSG_NOSIGNAL
3452					 *
3453					 * If we can use SO_NOSIGPIPE, then set sigpipe_so here
3454					 * and we're done.  Otherwise, set sigpipe_flag so that
3455					 * we will try MSG_NOSIGNAL on sends.  If we get an error
3456					 * with MSG_NOSIGNAL, we'll clear that flag and revert to
3457					 * signal masking.
3458					 *----------
3459					 */
3460					conn->sigpipe_so = false;
3461#ifdef MSG_NOSIGNAL
3462					conn->sigpipe_flag = true;
3463#else
3464					conn->sigpipe_flag = false;
3465#endif							/* MSG_NOSIGNAL */
3466
3467#ifdef SO_NOSIGPIPE
3468					optval = 1;
3469					if (setsockopt(conn->sock, SOL_SOCKET, SO_NOSIGPIPE,
3470								   (char *) &optval, sizeof(optval)) == 0)
3471					{
3472						conn->sigpipe_so = true;
3473						conn->sigpipe_flag = false;
3474					}
3475#endif							/* SO_NOSIGPIPE */
3476
3477					/*
3478					 * Start/make connection.  This should not block, since we
3479					 * are in nonblock mode.  If it does, well, too bad.
3480					 */
3481					if (connect(conn->sock, (struct sockaddr *) &addr_cur->addr.addr,
3482								addr_cur->addr.salen) < 0)
3483					{
3484						if (SOCK_ERRNO == EINPROGRESS ||
3485#ifdef WIN32
3486							SOCK_ERRNO == EWOULDBLOCK ||
3487#endif
3488							SOCK_ERRNO == EINTR)
3489						{
3490							/*
3491							 * This is fine - we're in non-blocking mode, and
3492							 * the connection is in progress.  Tell caller to
3493							 * wait for write-ready on socket.
3494							 */
3495							conn->status = CONNECTION_STARTED;
3496							return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
3497						}
3498						/* otherwise, trouble */
3499					}
3500					else
3501					{
3502						/*
3503						 * Hm, we're connected already --- seems the "nonblock
3504						 * connection" wasn't.  Advance the state machine and
3505						 * go do the next stuff.
3506						 */
3507						conn->status = CONNECTION_STARTED;
3508						goto keep_going;
3509					}
3510
3511					/*
3512					 * This connection failed.  Add the error report to
3513					 * conn->errorMessage, then try the next address if any.
3514					 */
3515					connectFailureMessage(conn, SOCK_ERRNO);
3516					conn->try_next_addr = true;
3517					goto keep_going;
3518				}
3519			}
3520
3521		case CONNECTION_STARTED:
3522			{
3523				socklen_t	optlen = sizeof(optval);
3524
3525				/*
3526				 * Write ready, since we've made it here, so the connection
3527				 * has been made ... or has failed.
3528				 */
3529
3530				/*
3531				 * Now check (using getsockopt) that there is not an error
3532				 * state waiting for us on the socket.
3533				 */
3534
3535				if (getsockopt(conn->sock, SOL_SOCKET, SO_ERROR,
3536							   (char *) &optval, &optlen) == -1)
3537				{
3538					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not get socket error status: %s",
3539											SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3540					goto error_return;
3541				}
3542				else if (optval != 0)
3543				{
3544					/*
3545					 * When using a nonblocking connect, we will typically see
3546					 * connect failures at this point, so provide a friendly
3547					 * error message.
3548					 */
3549					connectFailureMessage(conn, optval);
3550
3551					/*
3552					 * Try the next address if any, just as in the case where
3553					 * connect() returned failure immediately.
3554					 */
3555					conn->try_next_addr = true;
3556					goto keep_going;
3557				}
3558
3559				/* Fill in the client address */
3560				conn->laddr.salen = sizeof(conn->laddr.addr);
3561				if (getsockname(conn->sock,
3562								(struct sockaddr *) &conn->laddr.addr,
3563								&conn->laddr.salen) < 0)
3564				{
3565					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not get client address from socket: %s",
3566											SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3567					goto error_return;
3568				}
3569
3570				/*
3571				 * Implement requirepeer check, if requested and it's a
3572				 * Unix-domain socket.
3573				 */
3574				if (conn->requirepeer && conn->requirepeer[0] &&
3575					conn->raddr.addr.ss_family == AF_UNIX)
3576				{
3577#ifndef WIN32
3578					char	   *remote_username;
3579#endif
3580					uid_t		uid;
3581					gid_t		gid;
3582
3583					errno = 0;
3584					if (getpeereid(conn->sock, &uid, &gid) != 0)
3585					{
3586						/*
3587						 * Provide special error message if getpeereid is a
3588						 * stub
3589						 */
3590						if (errno == ENOSYS)
3591							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "requirepeer parameter is not supported on this platform");
3592						else
3593							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not get peer credentials: %s",
3594													strerror_r(errno, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3595						goto error_return;
3596					}
3597
3598#ifndef WIN32
3599					remote_username = pg_fe_getusername(uid,
3600														&conn->errorMessage);
3601					if (remote_username == NULL)
3602						goto error_return;	/* message already logged */
3603
3604					if (strcmp(remote_username, conn->requirepeer) != 0)
3605					{
3606						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "requirepeer specifies \"%s\", but actual peer user name is \"%s\"",
3607												conn->requirepeer, remote_username);
3608						free(remote_username);
3609						goto error_return;
3610					}
3611					free(remote_username);
3612#else							/* WIN32 */
3613					/* should have failed with ENOSYS above */
3614					Assert(false);
3615#endif							/* WIN32 */
3616				}
3617
3618				/*
3619				 * Make sure we can write before advancing to next step.
3620				 */
3621				conn->status = CONNECTION_MADE;
3622				return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
3623			}
3624
3625		case CONNECTION_MADE:
3626			{
3627				char	   *startpacket;
3628				int			packetlen;
3629
3630#ifdef ENABLE_GSS
3631
3632				/*
3633				 * If GSSAPI encryption is enabled, send a packet to the
3634				 * server asking for GSSAPI Encryption and proceed with GSSAPI
3635				 * handshake.  We will come back here after GSSAPI encryption
3636				 * has been established, with conn->gctx set.
3637				 */
3638				if (conn->current_enc_method == ENC_GSSAPI && !conn->gctx)
3639				{
3640					ProtocolVersion pv = pg_hton32(NEGOTIATE_GSS_CODE);
3641
3642					if (pqPacketSend(conn, 0, &pv, sizeof(pv)) != STATUS_OK)
3643					{
3644						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not send GSSAPI negotiation packet: %s",
3645												SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3646						goto error_return;
3647					}
3648
3649					/* Ok, wait for response */
3650					conn->status = CONNECTION_GSS_STARTUP;
3651					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3652				}
3653#endif
3654
3655#ifdef USE_SSL
3656
3657				/*
3658				 * If SSL is enabled, start the SSL negotiation. We will come
3659				 * back here after SSL encryption has been established, with
3660				 * ssl_in_use set.
3661				 */
3662				if (conn->current_enc_method == ENC_SSL && !conn->ssl_in_use)
3663				{
3664					/*
3665					 * If traditional postgres SSL negotiation is used, send
3666					 * the SSL request.  In direct negotiation, jump straight
3667					 * into the SSL handshake.
3668					 */
3669					if (conn->sslnegotiation[0] == 'p')
3670					{
3671						ProtocolVersion pv;
3672
3673						/*
3674						 * Send the SSL request packet.
3675						 *
3676						 * Theoretically, this could block, but it really
3677						 * shouldn't since we only got here if the socket is
3678						 * write-ready.
3679						 */
3680						pv = pg_hton32(NEGOTIATE_SSL_CODE);
3681						if (pqPacketSend(conn, 0, &pv, sizeof(pv)) != STATUS_OK)
3682						{
3683							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not send SSL negotiation packet: %s",
3684													SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3685							goto error_return;
3686						}
3687						/* Ok, wait for response */
3688						conn->status = CONNECTION_SSL_STARTUP;
3689						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3690					}
3691					else
3692					{
3693						Assert(conn->sslnegotiation[0] == 'd');
3694						conn->status = CONNECTION_SSL_STARTUP;
3695						return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
3696					}
3697				}
3698#endif							/* USE_SSL */
3699
3700				/*
3701				 * For cancel requests this is as far as we need to go in the
3702				 * connection establishment. Now we can actually send our
3703				 * cancellation request.
3704				 */
3705				if (conn->cancelRequest)
3706				{
3707					if (PQsendCancelRequest(conn) != STATUS_OK)
3708					{
3709						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not send cancel packet: %s",
3710												SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3711						goto error_return;
3712					}
3713					conn->status = CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE;
3714					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3715				}
3716
3717				/*
3718				 * We have now established encryption, or we are happy to
3719				 * proceed without.
3720				 */
3721
3722				/* Build the startup packet. */
3723				startpacket = pqBuildStartupPacket3(conn, &packetlen,
3724													EnvironmentOptions);
3725				if (!startpacket)
3726				{
3727					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "out of memory");
3728					goto error_return;
3729				}
3730
3731				/*
3732				 * Send the startup packet.
3733				 *
3734				 * Theoretically, this could block, but it really shouldn't
3735				 * since we only got here if the socket is write-ready.
3736				 */
3737				if (pqPacketSend(conn, 0, startpacket, packetlen) != STATUS_OK)
3738				{
3739					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "could not send startup packet: %s",
3740											SOCK_STRERROR(SOCK_ERRNO, sebuf, sizeof(sebuf)));
3741					free(startpacket);
3742					goto error_return;
3743				}
3744
3745				free(startpacket);
3746
3747				conn->status = CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE;
3748				return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3749			}
3750
3751			/*
3752			 * Handle SSL negotiation: wait for postmaster messages and
3753			 * respond as necessary.
3754			 */
3755		case CONNECTION_SSL_STARTUP:
3756			{
3757#ifdef USE_SSL
3758				PostgresPollingStatusType pollres;
3759
3760				/*
3761				 * On first time through with traditional SSL negotiation, get
3762				 * the postmaster's response to our SSLRequest packet. With
3763				 * sslnegotiation='direct', go straight to initiating SSL.
3764				 */
3765				if (!conn->ssl_in_use && conn->sslnegotiation[0] == 'p')
3766				{
3767					/*
3768					 * We use pqReadData here since it has the logic to
3769					 * distinguish no-data-yet from connection closure. Since
3770					 * conn->ssl isn't set, a plain recv() will occur.
3771					 */
3772					char		SSLok;
3773					int			rdresult;
3774
3775					rdresult = pqReadData(conn);
3776					if (rdresult < 0)
3777					{
3778						/* errorMessage is already filled in */
3779						goto error_return;
3780					}
3781					if (rdresult == 0)
3782					{
3783						/* caller failed to wait for data */
3784						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3785					}
3786					if (pqGetc(&SSLok, conn) < 0)
3787					{
3788						/* should not happen really */
3789						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3790					}
3791					if (SSLok == 'S')
3792					{
3793						if (conn->Pfdebug)
3794							pqTraceOutputCharResponse(conn, "SSLResponse",
3795													  SSLok);
3796						/* mark byte consumed */
3797						conn->inStart = conn->inCursor;
3798					}
3799					else if (SSLok == 'N')
3800					{
3801						if (conn->Pfdebug)
3802							pqTraceOutputCharResponse(conn, "SSLResponse",
3803													  SSLok);
3804						/* mark byte consumed */
3805						conn->inStart = conn->inCursor;
3806
3807						/*
3808						 * The connection is still valid, so if it's OK to
3809						 * continue without SSL, we can proceed using this
3810						 * connection.  Otherwise return with an error.
3811						 */
3812						ENCRYPTION_NEGOTIATION_FAILED(libpq_gettext("server does not support SSL, but SSL was required"));
3813					}
3814					else if (SSLok == 'E')
3815					{
3816						/*
3817						 * Server failure of some sort, such as failure to
3818						 * fork a backend process.  Don't bother retrieving
3819						 * the error message; we should not trust it as the
3820						 * server has not been authenticated yet.
3821						 */
3822						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "server sent an error response during SSL exchange");
3823						goto error_return;
3824					}
3825					else
3826					{
3827						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid response to SSL negotiation: %c",
3828												SSLok);
3829						goto error_return;
3830					}
3831				}
3832
3833				/*
3834				 * Begin or continue the SSL negotiation process.
3835				 */
3836				pollres = pqsecure_open_client(conn);
3837				if (pollres == PGRES_POLLING_OK)
3838				{
3839					/*
3840					 * At this point we should have no data already buffered.
3841					 * If we do, it was received before we performed the SSL
3842					 * handshake, so it wasn't encrypted and indeed may have
3843					 * been injected by a man-in-the-middle.
3844					 */
3845					if (conn->inCursor != conn->inEnd)
3846					{
3847						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received unencrypted data after SSL response");
3848						goto error_return;
3849					}
3850
3851					/* SSL handshake done, ready to send startup packet */
3852					conn->status = CONNECTION_MADE;
3853					return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
3854				}
3855				if (pollres == PGRES_POLLING_FAILED)
3856				{
3857					/*
3858					 * SSL handshake failed.  We will retry with a plaintext
3859					 * connection, if permitted by sslmode.
3860					 */
3861					CONNECTION_FAILED();
3862				}
3863				/* Else, return POLLING_READING or POLLING_WRITING status */
3864				return pollres;
3865#else							/* !USE_SSL */
3866				/* can't get here */
3867				goto error_return;
3868#endif							/* USE_SSL */
3869			}
3870
3871		case CONNECTION_GSS_STARTUP:
3872			{
3873#ifdef ENABLE_GSS
3874				PostgresPollingStatusType pollres;
3875
3876				/*
3877				 * If we haven't yet, get the postmaster's response to our
3878				 * negotiation packet
3879				 */
3880				if (!conn->gctx)
3881				{
3882					char		gss_ok;
3883					int			rdresult = pqReadData(conn);
3884
3885					if (rdresult < 0)
3886						/* pqReadData fills in error message */
3887						goto error_return;
3888					else if (rdresult == 0)
3889						/* caller failed to wait for data */
3890						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3891					if (pqGetc(&gss_ok, conn) < 0)
3892						/* shouldn't happen... */
3893						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
3894
3895					if (gss_ok == 'E')
3896					{
3897						/*
3898						 * Server failure of some sort, possibly protocol
3899						 * version support failure.  Don't bother retrieving
3900						 * the error message; we should not trust it anyway as
3901						 * the server has not authenticated yet.
3902						 *
3903						 * Note that unlike on an error response to
3904						 * SSLRequest, we allow falling back to SSL or
3905						 * plaintext connection here.  GSS support was
3906						 * introduced in PostgreSQL version 12, so an error
3907						 * response might mean that we are connecting to a
3908						 * pre-v12 server.
3909						 */
3910						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "server sent an error response during GSS encryption exchange");
3911						CONNECTION_FAILED();
3912					}
3913
3914					/* mark byte consumed */
3915					conn->inStart = conn->inCursor;
3916
3917					if (gss_ok == 'N')
3918					{
3919						if (conn->Pfdebug)
3920							pqTraceOutputCharResponse(conn, "GSSENCResponse",
3921													  gss_ok);
3922
3923						/*
3924						 * The connection is still valid, so if it's OK to
3925						 * continue without GSS, we can proceed using this
3926						 * connection.  Otherwise return with an error.
3927						 */
3928						ENCRYPTION_NEGOTIATION_FAILED(libpq_gettext("server doesn't support GSSAPI encryption, but it was required"));
3929					}
3930					else if (gss_ok != 'G')
3931					{
3932						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid response to GSSAPI negotiation: %c",
3933												gss_ok);
3934						goto error_return;
3935					}
3936
3937					if (conn->Pfdebug)
3938						pqTraceOutputCharResponse(conn, "GSSENCResponse",
3939												  gss_ok);
3940				}
3941
3942				/* Begin or continue GSSAPI negotiation */
3943				pollres = pqsecure_open_gss(conn);
3944				if (pollres == PGRES_POLLING_OK)
3945				{
3946					/*
3947					 * At this point we should have no data already buffered.
3948					 * If we do, it was received before we performed the GSS
3949					 * handshake, so it wasn't encrypted and indeed may have
3950					 * been injected by a man-in-the-middle.
3951					 */
3952					if (conn->inCursor != conn->inEnd)
3953					{
3954						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received unencrypted data after GSSAPI encryption response");
3955						goto error_return;
3956					}
3957
3958					/* All set for startup packet */
3959					conn->status = CONNECTION_MADE;
3960					return PGRES_POLLING_WRITING;
3961				}
3962				else if (pollres == PGRES_POLLING_FAILED)
3963				{
3964					/*
3965					 * GSS handshake failed.  We will retry with an SSL or
3966					 * plaintext connection, if permitted by the options.
3967					 */
3968					CONNECTION_FAILED();
3969				}
3970				/* Else, return POLLING_READING or POLLING_WRITING status */
3971				return pollres;
3972#else							/* !ENABLE_GSS */
3973				/* unreachable */
3974				goto error_return;
3975#endif							/* ENABLE_GSS */
3976			}
3977
3978			/*
3979			 * Handle authentication exchange: wait for postmaster messages
3980			 * and respond as necessary.
3981			 */
3982		case CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE:
3983			{
3984				char		beresp;
3985				int			msgLength;
3986				int			avail;
3987				AuthRequest areq;
3988				int			res;
3989				bool		async;
3990
3991				/*
3992				 * Scan the message from current point (note that if we find
3993				 * the message is incomplete, we will return without advancing
3994				 * inStart, and resume here next time).
3995				 */
3996				conn->inCursor = conn->inStart;
3997
3998				/* Read type byte */
3999				if (pqGetc(&beresp, conn))
4000				{
4001					/* We'll come back when there is more data */
4002					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4003				}
4004
4005				/*
4006				 * Validate message type: we expect only an authentication
4007				 * request, NegotiateProtocolVersion, or an error here.
4008				 * Anything else probably means it's not Postgres on the other
4009				 * end at all.
4010				 */
4011				if (beresp != PqMsg_AuthenticationRequest &&
4012					beresp != PqMsg_ErrorResponse &&
4013					beresp != PqMsg_NegotiateProtocolVersion)
4014				{
4015					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "expected authentication request from server, but received %c",
4016											beresp);
4017					goto error_return;
4018				}
4019
4020				/* Read message length word */
4021				if (pqGetInt(&msgLength, 4, conn))
4022				{
4023					/* We'll come back when there is more data */
4024					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4025				}
4026
4027				/*
4028				 * Try to validate message length before using it.
4029				 *
4030				 * Authentication requests can't be very large, although GSS
4031				 * auth requests may not be that small.  Same for
4032				 * NegotiateProtocolVersion.
4033				 *
4034				 * Errors can be a little larger, but not huge.  If we see a
4035				 * large apparent length in an error, it means we're really
4036				 * talking to a pre-3.0-protocol server; cope.  (Before
4037				 * version 14, the server also used the old protocol for
4038				 * errors that happened before processing the startup packet.)
4039				 */
4040				if (beresp == PqMsg_AuthenticationRequest &&
4041					(msgLength < 8 || msgLength > 2000))
4042				{
4043					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid authentication request");
4044					goto error_return;
4045				}
4046				if (beresp == PqMsg_NegotiateProtocolVersion &&
4047					(msgLength < 8 || msgLength > 2000))
4048				{
4049					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid protocol negotiation message");
4050					goto error_return;
4051				}
4052
4053#define MAX_ERRLEN 30000
4054				if (beresp == PqMsg_ErrorResponse &&
4055					(msgLength < 8 || msgLength > MAX_ERRLEN))
4056				{
4057					/* Handle error from a pre-3.0 server */
4058					conn->inCursor = conn->inStart + 1; /* reread data */
4059					if (pqGets_append(&conn->errorMessage, conn))
4060					{
4061						/*
4062						 * We may not have authenticated the server yet, so
4063						 * don't let the buffer grow forever.
4064						 */
4065						avail = conn->inEnd - conn->inCursor;
4066						if (avail > MAX_ERRLEN)
4067						{
4068							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid error message");
4069							goto error_return;
4070						}
4071
4072						/* We'll come back when there is more data */
4073						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4074					}
4075					/* OK, we read the message; mark data consumed */
4076					pqParseDone(conn, conn->inCursor);
4077
4078					/*
4079					 * Before 7.2, the postmaster didn't always end its
4080					 * messages with a newline, so add one if needed to
4081					 * conform to libpq conventions.
4082					 */
4083					if (conn->errorMessage.len == 0 ||
4084						conn->errorMessage.data[conn->errorMessage.len - 1] != '\n')
4085					{
4086						appendPQExpBufferChar(&conn->errorMessage, '\n');
4087					}
4088
4089					goto error_return;
4090				}
4091#undef MAX_ERRLEN
4092
4093				/*
4094				 * Can't process if message body isn't all here yet.
4095				 *
4096				 * After this check passes, any further EOF during parsing
4097				 * implies that the server sent a bad/truncated message.
4098				 * Reading more bytes won't help in that case, so don't return
4099				 * PGRES_POLLING_READING after this point.
4100				 */
4101				msgLength -= 4;
4102				avail = conn->inEnd - conn->inCursor;
4103				if (avail < msgLength)
4104				{
4105					/*
4106					 * Before returning, try to enlarge the input buffer if
4107					 * needed to hold the whole message; see notes in
4108					 * pqParseInput3.
4109					 */
4110					if (pqCheckInBufferSpace(conn->inCursor + (size_t) msgLength,
4111											 conn))
4112						goto error_return;
4113					/* We'll come back when there is more data */
4114					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4115				}
4116
4117				/* Handle errors. */
4118				if (beresp == PqMsg_ErrorResponse)
4119				{
4120					if (pqGetErrorNotice3(conn, true))
4121					{
4122						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid error message");
4123						goto error_return;
4124					}
4125					/* OK, we read the message; mark data consumed */
4126					pqParseDone(conn, conn->inCursor);
4127
4128					/*
4129					 * If error is "cannot connect now", try the next host if
4130					 * any (but we don't want to consider additional addresses
4131					 * for this host, nor is there much point in changing SSL
4132					 * or GSS mode).  This is helpful when dealing with
4133					 * standby servers that might not be in hot-standby state.
4134					 */
4135					if (strcmp(conn->last_sqlstate,
4136							   ERRCODE_CANNOT_CONNECT_NOW) == 0)
4137					{
4138						conn->try_next_host = true;
4139						goto keep_going;
4140					}
4141
4142					/* Check to see if we should mention pgpassfile */
4143					pgpassfileWarning(conn);
4144
4145					CONNECTION_FAILED();
4146				}
4147				/* Handle NegotiateProtocolVersion */
4148				else if (beresp == PqMsg_NegotiateProtocolVersion)
4149				{
4150					if (conn->pversion_negotiated)
4151					{
4152						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received duplicate protocol negotiation message");
4153						goto error_return;
4154					}
4155					if (pqGetNegotiateProtocolVersion3(conn))
4156					{
4157						/* pqGetNegotiateProtocolVersion3 set error already */
4158						goto error_return;
4159					}
4160					conn->pversion_negotiated = true;
4161
4162					/* OK, we read the message; mark data consumed */
4163					pqParseDone(conn, conn->inCursor);
4164
4165					goto keep_going;
4166				}
4167
4168				/* It is an authentication request. */
4169				conn->auth_req_received = true;
4170
4171				/* Get the type of request. */
4172				if (pqGetInt((int *) &areq, 4, conn))
4173				{
4174					/* can't happen because we checked the length already */
4175					libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "received invalid authentication request");
4176					goto error_return;
4177				}
4178				msgLength -= 4;
4179
4180				/*
4181				 * Process the rest of the authentication request message, and
4182				 * respond to it if necessary.
4183				 *
4184				 * Note that conn->pghost must be non-NULL if we are going to
4185				 * avoid the Kerberos code doing a hostname look-up.
4186				 */
4187				res = pg_fe_sendauth(areq, msgLength, conn, &async);
4188
4189				if (async && (res == STATUS_OK))
4190				{
4191					/*
4192					 * We'll come back later once we're ready to respond.
4193					 * Don't consume the request yet.
4194					 */
4195					conn->status = CONNECTION_AUTHENTICATING;
4196					goto keep_going;
4197				}
4198
4199				/*
4200				 * OK, we have processed the message; mark data consumed.  We
4201				 * don't call pqParseDone here because we already traced this
4202				 * message inside pg_fe_sendauth.
4203				 */
4204				conn->inStart = conn->inCursor;
4205
4206				if (res != STATUS_OK)
4207				{
4208					/*
4209					 * OAuth connections may perform two-step discovery, where
4210					 * the first connection is a dummy.
4211					 */
4212					if (conn->sasl == &pg_oauth_mech && conn->oauth_want_retry)
4213					{
4214						need_new_connection = true;
4215						goto keep_going;
4216					}
4217
4218					goto error_return;
4219				}
4220
4221				/*
4222				 * Just make sure that any data sent by pg_fe_sendauth is
4223				 * flushed out.  Although this theoretically could block, it
4224				 * really shouldn't since we don't send large auth responses.
4225				 */
4226				if (pqFlush(conn))
4227					goto error_return;
4228
4229				if (areq == AUTH_REQ_OK)
4230				{
4231					/* We are done with authentication exchange */
4232					conn->status = CONNECTION_AUTH_OK;
4233
4234					/*
4235					 * Set asyncStatus so that PQgetResult will think that
4236					 * what comes back next is the result of a query.  See
4237					 * below.
4238					 */
4239					conn->asyncStatus = PGASYNC_BUSY;
4240				}
4241
4242				/* Look to see if we have more data yet. */
4243				goto keep_going;
4244			}
4245
4246		case CONNECTION_AUTHENTICATING:
4247			{
4248				PostgresPollingStatusType status;
4249
4250				if (!conn->async_auth || !conn->cleanup_async_auth)
4251				{
4252					/* programmer error; should not happen */
4253					libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4254											"internal error: async authentication has no handler");
4255					goto error_return;
4256				}
4257
4258				/* Drive some external authentication work. */
4259				status = conn->async_auth(conn);
4260
4261				if (status == PGRES_POLLING_FAILED)
4262					goto error_return;
4263
4264				if (status == PGRES_POLLING_OK)
4265				{
4266					/* Done. Tear down the async implementation. */
4267					conn->cleanup_async_auth(conn);
4268					conn->cleanup_async_auth = NULL;
4269
4270					/*
4271					 * Cleanup must unset altsock, both as an indication that
4272					 * it's been released, and to stop pqSocketCheck from
4273					 * looking at the wrong socket after async auth is done.
4274					 */
4275					if (conn->altsock != PGINVALID_SOCKET)
4276					{
4277						Assert(false);
4278						libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4279												"internal error: async cleanup did not release polling socket");
4280						goto error_return;
4281					}
4282
4283					/*
4284					 * Reenter the authentication exchange with the server. We
4285					 * didn't consume the message that started external
4286					 * authentication, so it'll be reprocessed as if we just
4287					 * received it.
4288					 */
4289					conn->status = CONNECTION_AWAITING_RESPONSE;
4290
4291					goto keep_going;
4292				}
4293
4294				/*
4295				 * Caller needs to poll some more. conn->async_auth() should
4296				 * have assigned an altsock to poll on.
4297				 */
4298				if (conn->altsock == PGINVALID_SOCKET)
4299				{
4300					Assert(false);
4301					libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4302											"internal error: async authentication did not set a socket for polling");
4303					goto error_return;
4304				}
4305
4306				return status;
4307			}
4308
4309		case CONNECTION_AUTH_OK:
4310			{
4311				/*
4312				 * Now we expect to hear from the backend. A ReadyForQuery
4313				 * message indicates that startup is successful, but we might
4314				 * also get an Error message indicating failure. (Notice
4315				 * messages indicating nonfatal warnings are also allowed by
4316				 * the protocol, as are ParameterStatus and BackendKeyData
4317				 * messages.) Easiest way to handle this is to let
4318				 * PQgetResult() read the messages. We just have to fake it
4319				 * out about the state of the connection, by setting
4320				 * asyncStatus = PGASYNC_BUSY (done above).
4321				 */
4322
4323				if (PQisBusy(conn))
4324					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4325
4326				res = PQgetResult(conn);
4327
4328				/*
4329				 * NULL return indicating we have gone to IDLE state is
4330				 * expected
4331				 */
4332				if (res)
4333				{
4334					if (res->resultStatus != PGRES_FATAL_ERROR)
4335						libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "unexpected message from server during startup");
4336					else if (conn->send_appname &&
4337							 (conn->appname || conn->fbappname))
4338					{
4339						/*
4340						 * If we tried to send application_name, check to see
4341						 * if the error is about that --- pre-9.0 servers will
4342						 * reject it at this stage of the process.  If so,
4343						 * close the connection and retry without sending
4344						 * application_name.  We could possibly get a false
4345						 * SQLSTATE match here and retry uselessly, but there
4346						 * seems no great harm in that; we'll just get the
4347						 * same error again if it's unrelated.
4348						 */
4349						const char *sqlstate;
4350
4351						sqlstate = PQresultErrorField(res, PG_DIAG_SQLSTATE);
4352						if (sqlstate &&
4353							strcmp(sqlstate, ERRCODE_APPNAME_UNKNOWN) == 0)
4354						{
4355							PQclear(res);
4356							conn->send_appname = false;
4357							need_new_connection = true;
4358							goto keep_going;
4359						}
4360					}
4361
4362					/*
4363					 * if the resultStatus is FATAL, then conn->errorMessage
4364					 * already has a copy of the error; needn't copy it back.
4365					 * But add a newline if it's not there already, since
4366					 * postmaster error messages may not have one.
4367					 */
4368					if (conn->errorMessage.len <= 0 ||
4369						conn->errorMessage.data[conn->errorMessage.len - 1] != '\n')
4370						appendPQExpBufferChar(&conn->errorMessage, '\n');
4371					PQclear(res);
4372					goto error_return;
4373				}
4374
4375				/* Almost there now ... */
4376				conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET;
4377				goto keep_going;
4378			}
4379
4380		case CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET:
4381			{
4382				/*
4383				 * If a read-write, read-only, primary, or standby connection
4384				 * is required, see if we have one.
4385				 */
4386				if (conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_READ_WRITE ||
4387					conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_READ_ONLY)
4388				{
4389					bool		read_only_server;
4390
4391					/*
4392					 * If the server didn't report
4393					 * "default_transaction_read_only" or "in_hot_standby" at
4394					 * startup, we must determine its state by sending the
4395					 * query "SHOW transaction_read_only".  This GUC exists in
4396					 * all server versions that support 3.0 protocol.
4397					 */
4398					if (conn->default_transaction_read_only == PG_BOOL_UNKNOWN ||
4399						conn->in_hot_standby == PG_BOOL_UNKNOWN)
4400					{
4401						/*
4402						 * We use PQsendQueryContinue so that
4403						 * conn->errorMessage does not get cleared.  We need
4404						 * to preserve any error messages related to previous
4405						 * hosts we have tried and failed to connect to.
4406						 */
4407						conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4408						if (!PQsendQueryContinue(conn,
4409												 "SHOW transaction_read_only"))
4410							goto error_return;
4411						/* We'll return to this state when we have the answer */
4412						conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_WRITABLE;
4413						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4414					}
4415
4416					/* OK, we can make the test */
4417					read_only_server =
4418						(conn->default_transaction_read_only == PG_BOOL_YES ||
4419						 conn->in_hot_standby == PG_BOOL_YES);
4420
4421					if ((conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_READ_WRITE) ?
4422						read_only_server : !read_only_server)
4423					{
4424						/* Wrong server state, reject and try the next host */
4425						if (conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_READ_WRITE)
4426							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "session is read-only");
4427						else
4428							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "session is not read-only");
4429
4430						/* Close connection politely. */
4431						conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4432						sendTerminateConn(conn);
4433
4434						/*
4435						 * Try next host if any, but we don't want to consider
4436						 * additional addresses for this host.
4437						 */
4438						conn->try_next_host = true;
4439						goto keep_going;
4440					}
4441				}
4442				else if (conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_PRIMARY ||
4443						 conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_STANDBY ||
4444						 conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_PREFER_STANDBY)
4445				{
4446					/*
4447					 * If the server didn't report "in_hot_standby" at
4448					 * startup, we must determine its state by sending the
4449					 * query "SELECT pg_catalog.pg_is_in_recovery()".  Servers
4450					 * before 9.0 don't have that function, but by the same
4451					 * token they don't have any standby mode, so we may just
4452					 * assume the result.
4453					 */
4454					if (conn->sversion < 90000)
4455						conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_NO;
4456
4457					if (conn->in_hot_standby == PG_BOOL_UNKNOWN)
4458					{
4459						/*
4460						 * We use PQsendQueryContinue so that
4461						 * conn->errorMessage does not get cleared.  We need
4462						 * to preserve any error messages related to previous
4463						 * hosts we have tried and failed to connect to.
4464						 */
4465						conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4466						if (!PQsendQueryContinue(conn,
4467												 "SELECT pg_catalog.pg_is_in_recovery()"))
4468							goto error_return;
4469						/* We'll return to this state when we have the answer */
4470						conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_STANDBY;
4471						return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4472					}
4473
4474					/* OK, we can make the test */
4475					if ((conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_PRIMARY) ?
4476						(conn->in_hot_standby == PG_BOOL_YES) :
4477						(conn->in_hot_standby == PG_BOOL_NO))
4478					{
4479						/* Wrong server state, reject and try the next host */
4480						if (conn->target_server_type == SERVER_TYPE_PRIMARY)
4481							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "server is in hot standby mode");
4482						else
4483							libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "server is not in hot standby mode");
4484
4485						/* Close connection politely. */
4486						conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4487						sendTerminateConn(conn);
4488
4489						/*
4490						 * Try next host if any, but we don't want to consider
4491						 * additional addresses for this host.
4492						 */
4493						conn->try_next_host = true;
4494						goto keep_going;
4495					}
4496				}
4497
4498				/* Don't hold onto any OAuth tokens longer than necessary. */
4499				pqClearOAuthToken(conn);
4500
4501				/*
4502				 * For non cancel requests we can release the address list
4503				 * now. For cancel requests we never actually resolve
4504				 * addresses and instead the addrinfo exists for the lifetime
4505				 * of the connection.
4506				 */
4507				if (!conn->cancelRequest)
4508					release_conn_addrinfo(conn);
4509
4510				/*
4511				 * Contents of conn->errorMessage are no longer interesting
4512				 * (and it seems some clients expect it to be empty after a
4513				 * successful connection).
4514				 */
4515				pqClearConnErrorState(conn);
4516
4517				/* We are open for business! */
4518				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4519				return PGRES_POLLING_OK;
4520			}
4521
4522		case CONNECTION_CONSUME:
4523			{
4524				/*
4525				 * This state just makes sure the connection is idle after
4526				 * we've obtained the result of a SHOW or SELECT query.  Once
4527				 * we're clear, return to CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET state to
4528				 * decide what to do next.  We must transiently set status =
4529				 * CONNECTION_OK in order to use the result-consuming
4530				 * subroutines.
4531				 */
4532				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4533				if (!PQconsumeInput(conn))
4534					goto error_return;
4535
4536				if (PQisBusy(conn))
4537				{
4538					conn->status = CONNECTION_CONSUME;
4539					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4540				}
4541
4542				/* Call PQgetResult() again until we get a NULL result */
4543				res = PQgetResult(conn);
4544				if (res != NULL)
4545				{
4546					PQclear(res);
4547					conn->status = CONNECTION_CONSUME;
4548					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4549				}
4550
4551				conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET;
4552				goto keep_going;
4553			}
4554
4555		case CONNECTION_CHECK_WRITABLE:
4556			{
4557				/*
4558				 * Waiting for result of "SHOW transaction_read_only".  We
4559				 * must transiently set status = CONNECTION_OK in order to use
4560				 * the result-consuming subroutines.
4561				 */
4562				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4563				if (!PQconsumeInput(conn))
4564					goto error_return;
4565
4566				if (PQisBusy(conn))
4567				{
4568					conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_WRITABLE;
4569					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4570				}
4571
4572				res = PQgetResult(conn);
4573				if (res && PQresultStatus(res) == PGRES_TUPLES_OK &&
4574					PQntuples(res) == 1)
4575				{
4576					char	   *val = PQgetvalue(res, 0, 0);
4577
4578					/*
4579					 * "transaction_read_only = on" proves that at least one
4580					 * of default_transaction_read_only and in_hot_standby is
4581					 * on, but we don't actually know which.  We don't care
4582					 * though for the purpose of identifying a read-only
4583					 * session, so satisfy the CONNECTION_CHECK_TARGET code by
4584					 * claiming they are both on.  On the other hand, if it's
4585					 * a read-write session, they are certainly both off.
4586					 */
4587					if (strncmp(val, "on", 2) == 0)
4588					{
4589						conn->default_transaction_read_only = PG_BOOL_YES;
4590						conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_YES;
4591					}
4592					else
4593					{
4594						conn->default_transaction_read_only = PG_BOOL_NO;
4595						conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_NO;
4596					}
4597					PQclear(res);
4598
4599					/* Finish reading messages before continuing */
4600					conn->status = CONNECTION_CONSUME;
4601					goto keep_going;
4602				}
4603
4604				/* Something went wrong with "SHOW transaction_read_only". */
4605				PQclear(res);
4606
4607				/* Append error report to conn->errorMessage. */
4608				libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "\"%s\" failed",
4609										"SHOW transaction_read_only");
4610
4611				/* Close connection politely. */
4612				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4613				sendTerminateConn(conn);
4614
4615				/* Try next host. */
4616				conn->try_next_host = true;
4617				goto keep_going;
4618			}
4619
4620		case CONNECTION_CHECK_STANDBY:
4621			{
4622				/*
4623				 * Waiting for result of "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery()".  We
4624				 * must transiently set status = CONNECTION_OK in order to use
4625				 * the result-consuming subroutines.
4626				 */
4627				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4628				if (!PQconsumeInput(conn))
4629					goto error_return;
4630
4631				if (PQisBusy(conn))
4632				{
4633					conn->status = CONNECTION_CHECK_STANDBY;
4634					return PGRES_POLLING_READING;
4635				}
4636
4637				res = PQgetResult(conn);
4638				if (res && PQresultStatus(res) == PGRES_TUPLES_OK &&
4639					PQntuples(res) == 1)
4640				{
4641					char	   *val = PQgetvalue(res, 0, 0);
4642
4643					if (strncmp(val, "t", 1) == 0)
4644						conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_YES;
4645					else
4646						conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_NO;
4647					PQclear(res);
4648
4649					/* Finish reading messages before continuing */
4650					conn->status = CONNECTION_CONSUME;
4651					goto keep_going;
4652				}
4653
4654				/* Something went wrong with "SELECT pg_is_in_recovery()". */
4655				PQclear(res);
4656
4657				/* Append error report to conn->errorMessage. */
4658				libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "\"%s\" failed",
4659										"SELECT pg_is_in_recovery()");
4660
4661				/* Close connection politely. */
4662				conn->status = CONNECTION_OK;
4663				sendTerminateConn(conn);
4664
4665				/* Try next host. */
4666				conn->try_next_host = true;
4667				goto keep_going;
4668			}
4669
4670		default:
4671			libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4672									"invalid connection state %d, probably indicative of memory corruption",
4673									conn->status);
4674			goto error_return;
4675	}
4676
4677	/* Unreachable */
4678
4679error_return:
4680
4681	/*
4682	 * We used to close the socket at this point, but that makes it awkward
4683	 * for those above us if they wish to remove this socket from their own
4684	 * records (an fd_set for example).  We'll just have this socket closed
4685	 * when PQfinish is called (which is compulsory even after an error, since
4686	 * the connection structure must be freed).
4687	 */
4688	conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
4689	return PGRES_POLLING_FAILED;
4690}
4691
4692/*
4693 * Initialize the state machine for negotiating encryption
4694 */
4695static bool
4696init_allowed_encryption_methods(PGconn *conn)
4697{
4698	if (conn->raddr.addr.ss_family == AF_UNIX)
4699	{
4700		/* Don't request SSL or GSSAPI over Unix sockets */
4701		conn->allowed_enc_methods &= ~(ENC_SSL | ENC_GSSAPI);
4702
4703		/*
4704		 * XXX: we probably should not do this. sslmode=require works
4705		 * differently
4706		 */
4707		if (conn->gssencmode[0] == 'r')
4708		{
4709			libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4710									"GSSAPI encryption required but it is not supported over a local socket");
4711			conn->allowed_enc_methods = 0;
4712			conn->current_enc_method = ENC_ERROR;
4713			return false;
4714		}
4715
4716		conn->allowed_enc_methods = ENC_PLAINTEXT;
4717		conn->current_enc_method = ENC_PLAINTEXT;
4718		return true;
4719	}
4720
4721	/* initialize based on sslmode and gssencmode */
4722	conn->allowed_enc_methods = 0;
4723
4724#ifdef USE_SSL
4725	/* sslmode anything but 'disable', and GSSAPI not required */
4726	if (conn->sslmode[0] != 'd' && conn->gssencmode[0] != 'r')
4727	{
4728		conn->allowed_enc_methods |= ENC_SSL;
4729	}
4730#endif
4731
4732#ifdef ENABLE_GSS
4733	if (conn->gssencmode[0] != 'd')
4734		conn->allowed_enc_methods |= ENC_GSSAPI;
4735#endif
4736
4737	if ((conn->sslmode[0] == 'd' || conn->sslmode[0] == 'p' || conn->sslmode[0] == 'a') &&
4738		(conn->gssencmode[0] == 'd' || conn->gssencmode[0] == 'p'))
4739	{
4740		conn->allowed_enc_methods |= ENC_PLAINTEXT;
4741	}
4742
4743	return select_next_encryption_method(conn, false);
4744}
4745
4746/*
4747 * Out-of-line portion of the ENCRYPTION_NEGOTIATION_FAILED() macro in the
4748 * PQconnectPoll state machine.
4749 *
4750 * Return value:
4751 *  0: connection failed and we are out of encryption methods to try. return an error
4752 *  1: Retry with next connection method. The TCP connection is still valid and in
4753 *     known state, so we can proceed with the negotiating next method without
4754 *     reconnecting.
4755 *  2: Disconnect, and retry with next connection method.
4756 *
4757 * conn->current_enc_method is updated to the next method to try.
4758 */
4759#if defined(USE_SSL) || defined(ENABLE_GSS)
4760static int
4761encryption_negotiation_failed(PGconn *conn)
4762{
4763	Assert((conn->failed_enc_methods & conn->current_enc_method) == 0);
4764	conn->failed_enc_methods |= conn->current_enc_method;
4765
4766	if (select_next_encryption_method(conn, true))
4767	{
4768		/* An existing connection cannot be reused for direct SSL */
4769		if (conn->current_enc_method == ENC_SSL && conn->sslnegotiation[0] == 'd')
4770			return 2;
4771		else
4772			return 1;
4773	}
4774	else
4775		return 0;
4776}
4777#endif
4778
4779/*
4780 * Out-of-line portion of the CONNECTION_FAILED() macro
4781 *
4782 * Returns true, if we should reconnect and retry with a different encryption
4783 * method.  conn->current_enc_method is updated to the next method to try.
4784 */
4785static bool
4786connection_failed(PGconn *conn)
4787{
4788	Assert((conn->failed_enc_methods & conn->current_enc_method) == 0);
4789	conn->failed_enc_methods |= conn->current_enc_method;
4790
4791	return select_next_encryption_method(conn, false);
4792}
4793
4794/*
4795 * Choose the next encryption method to try. If this is a retry,
4796 * conn->failed_enc_methods has already been updated. The function sets
4797 * conn->current_enc_method to the next method to try. Returns false if no
4798 * encryption methods remain.
4799 */
4800static bool
4801select_next_encryption_method(PGconn *conn, bool have_valid_connection)
4802{
4803	int			remaining_methods;
4804
4805#define SELECT_NEXT_METHOD(method) \
4806	do { \
4807		if ((remaining_methods & method) != 0) \
4808		{ \
4809			conn->current_enc_method = method; \
4810			return true; \
4811		} \
4812	} while (false)
4813
4814	remaining_methods = conn->allowed_enc_methods & ~conn->failed_enc_methods;
4815
4816	/*
4817	 * Try GSSAPI before SSL
4818	 */
4819#ifdef ENABLE_GSS
4820	if ((remaining_methods & ENC_GSSAPI) != 0)
4821	{
4822		/*
4823		 * If GSSAPI encryption is enabled, then call pg_GSS_have_cred_cache()
4824		 * which will return true if we can acquire credentials (and give us a
4825		 * handle to use in conn->gcred), and then send a packet to the server
4826		 * asking for GSSAPI Encryption (and skip past SSL negotiation and
4827		 * regular startup below).
4828		 */
4829		if (!conn->gctx)
4830		{
4831			if (!pg_GSS_have_cred_cache(&conn->gcred))
4832			{
4833				conn->allowed_enc_methods &= ~ENC_GSSAPI;
4834				remaining_methods &= ~ENC_GSSAPI;
4835
4836				if (conn->gssencmode[0] == 'r')
4837				{
4838					libpq_append_conn_error(conn,
4839											"GSSAPI encryption required but no credential cache");
4840				}
4841			}
4842		}
4843	}
4844
4845	SELECT_NEXT_METHOD(ENC_GSSAPI);
4846#endif
4847
4848	/*
4849	 * The order between SSL encryption and plaintext depends on sslmode. With
4850	 * sslmode=allow, try plaintext connection before SSL. With
4851	 * sslmode=prefer, it's the other way round. With other modes, we only try
4852	 * plaintext or SSL connections so the order they're listed here doesn't
4853	 * matter.
4854	 */
4855	if (conn->sslmode[0] == 'a')
4856		SELECT_NEXT_METHOD(ENC_PLAINTEXT);
4857
4858	SELECT_NEXT_METHOD(ENC_SSL);
4859
4860	if (conn->sslmode[0] != 'a')
4861		SELECT_NEXT_METHOD(ENC_PLAINTEXT);
4862
4863	/* No more options */
4864	conn->current_enc_method = ENC_ERROR;
4865	return false;
4866#undef SELECT_NEXT_METHOD
4867}
4868
4869/*
4870 * internal_ping
4871 *		Determine if a server is running and if we can connect to it.
4872 *
4873 * The argument is a connection that's been started, but not completed.
4874 */
4875static PGPing
4876internal_ping(PGconn *conn)
4877{
4878	/* Say "no attempt" if we never got to PQconnectPoll */
4879	if (!conn || !conn->options_valid)
4880		return PQPING_NO_ATTEMPT;
4881
4882	/* Attempt to complete the connection */
4883	if (conn->status != CONNECTION_BAD)
4884		(void) pqConnectDBComplete(conn);
4885
4886	/* Definitely OK if we succeeded */
4887	if (conn->status != CONNECTION_BAD)
4888		return PQPING_OK;
4889
4890	/*
4891	 * Here begins the interesting part of "ping": determine the cause of the
4892	 * failure in sufficient detail to decide what to return.  We do not want
4893	 * to report that the server is not up just because we didn't have a valid
4894	 * password, for example.  In fact, any sort of authentication request
4895	 * implies the server is up.  (We need this check since the libpq side of
4896	 * things might have pulled the plug on the connection before getting an
4897	 * error as such from the postmaster.)
4898	 */
4899	if (conn->auth_req_received)
4900		return PQPING_OK;
4901
4902	/*
4903	 * If we failed to get any ERROR response from the postmaster, report
4904	 * PQPING_NO_RESPONSE.  This result could be somewhat misleading for a
4905	 * pre-7.4 server, since it won't send back a SQLSTATE, but those are long
4906	 * out of support.  Another corner case where the server could return a
4907	 * failure without a SQLSTATE is fork failure, but PQPING_NO_RESPONSE
4908	 * isn't totally unreasonable for that anyway.  We expect that every other
4909	 * failure case in a modern server will produce a report with a SQLSTATE.
4910	 *
4911	 * NOTE: whenever we get around to making libpq generate SQLSTATEs for
4912	 * client-side errors, we should either not store those into
4913	 * last_sqlstate, or add an extra flag so we can tell client-side errors
4914	 * apart from server-side ones.
4915	 */
4916	if (strlen(conn->last_sqlstate) != 5)
4917		return PQPING_NO_RESPONSE;
4918
4919	/*
4920	 * Report PQPING_REJECT if server says it's not accepting connections.
4921	 */
4922	if (strcmp(conn->last_sqlstate, ERRCODE_CANNOT_CONNECT_NOW) == 0)
4923		return PQPING_REJECT;
4924
4925	/*
4926	 * Any other SQLSTATE can be taken to indicate that the server is up.
4927	 * Presumably it didn't like our username, password, or database name; or
4928	 * perhaps it had some transient failure, but that should not be taken as
4929	 * meaning "it's down".
4930	 */
4931	return PQPING_OK;
4932}
4933
4934
4935/*
4936 * pqMakeEmptyPGconn
4937 *	 - create a PGconn data structure with (as yet) no interesting data
4938 */
4939PGconn *
4940pqMakeEmptyPGconn(void)
4941{
4942	PGconn	   *conn;
4943
4944#ifdef WIN32
4945
4946	/*
4947	 * Make sure socket support is up and running in this process.
4948	 *
4949	 * Note: the Windows documentation says that we should eventually do a
4950	 * matching WSACleanup() call, but experience suggests that that is at
4951	 * least as likely to cause problems as fix them.  So we don't.
4952	 */
4953	static bool wsastartup_done = false;
4954
4955	if (!wsastartup_done)
4956	{
4957		WSADATA		wsaData;
4958
4959		if (WSAStartup(MAKEWORD(2, 2), &wsaData) != 0)
4960			return NULL;
4961		wsastartup_done = true;
4962	}
4963
4964	/* Forget any earlier error */
4965	WSASetLastError(0);
4966#endif							/* WIN32 */
4967
4968	conn = (PGconn *) malloc(sizeof(PGconn));
4969	if (conn == NULL)
4970		return conn;
4971
4972	/* Zero all pointers and booleans */
4973	MemSet(conn, 0, sizeof(PGconn));
4974
4975	/* install default notice hooks */
4976	conn->noticeHooks.noticeRec = defaultNoticeReceiver;
4977	conn->noticeHooks.noticeProc = defaultNoticeProcessor;
4978
4979	conn->status = CONNECTION_BAD;
4980	conn->asyncStatus = PGASYNC_IDLE;
4981	conn->pipelineStatus = PQ_PIPELINE_OFF;
4982	conn->xactStatus = PQTRANS_IDLE;
4983	conn->options_valid = false;
4984	conn->nonblocking = false;
4985	conn->client_encoding = PG_SQL_ASCII;
4986	conn->std_strings = false;	/* unless server says differently */
4987	conn->default_transaction_read_only = PG_BOOL_UNKNOWN;
4988	conn->in_hot_standby = PG_BOOL_UNKNOWN;
4989	conn->scram_sha_256_iterations = SCRAM_SHA_256_DEFAULT_ITERATIONS;
4990	conn->verbosity = PQERRORS_DEFAULT;
4991	conn->show_context = PQSHOW_CONTEXT_ERRORS;
4992	conn->sock = PGINVALID_SOCKET;
4993	conn->altsock = PGINVALID_SOCKET;
4994	conn->Pfdebug = NULL;
4995
4996	/*
4997	 * We try to send at least 8K at a time, which is the usual size of pipe
4998	 * buffers on Unix systems.  That way, when we are sending a large amount
4999	 * of data, we avoid incurring extra kernel context swaps for partial
5000	 * bufferloads.  The output buffer is initially made 16K in size, and we
5001	 * try to dump it after accumulating 8K.
5002	 *
5003	 * With the same goal of minimizing context swaps, the input buffer will
5004	 * be enlarged anytime it has less than 8K free, so we initially allocate
5005	 * twice that.
5006	 */
5007	conn->inBufSize = 16 * 1024;
5008	conn->inBuffer = (char *) malloc(conn->inBufSize);
5009	conn->outBufSize = 16 * 1024;
5010	conn->outBuffer = (char *) malloc(conn->outBufSize);
5011	conn->rowBufLen = 32;
5012	conn->rowBuf = (PGdataValue *) malloc(conn->rowBufLen * sizeof(PGdataValue));
5013	initPQExpBuffer(&conn->errorMessage);
5014	initPQExpBuffer(&conn->workBuffer);
5015
5016	if (conn->inBuffer == NULL ||
5017		conn->outBuffer == NULL ||
5018		conn->rowBuf == NULL ||
5019		PQExpBufferBroken(&conn->errorMessage) ||
5020		PQExpBufferBroken(&conn->workBuffer))
5021	{
5022		/* out of memory already :-( */
5023		freePGconn(conn);
5024		conn = NULL;
5025	}
5026
5027	return conn;
5028}
5029
5030/*
5031 * freePGconn
5032 *	 - free an idle (closed) PGconn data structure
5033 *
5034 * NOTE: this should not overlap any functionality with pqClosePGconn().
5035 * Clearing/resetting of transient state belongs there; what we do here is
5036 * release data that is to be held for the life of the PGconn structure.
5037 * If a value ought to be cleared/freed during PQreset(), do it there not here.
5038 */
5039static void
5040freePGconn(PGconn *conn)
5041{
5042	/* let any event procs clean up their state data */
5043	for (int i = 0; i < conn->nEvents; i++)
5044	{
5045		PGEventConnDestroy evt;
5046
5047		evt.conn = conn;
5048		(void) conn->events[i].proc(PGEVT_CONNDESTROY, &evt,
5049									conn->events[i].passThrough);
5050		free(conn->events[i].name);
5051	}
5052
5053	/* free everything not freed in pqClosePGconn */
5054	free(conn->pghost);
5055	free(conn->pghostaddr);
5056	free(conn->pgport);
5057	free(conn->connect_timeout);
5058	free(conn->pgtcp_user_timeout);
5059	free(conn->client_encoding_initial);
5060	free(conn->pgoptions);
5061	free(conn->appname);
5062	free(conn->fbappname);
5063	free(conn->dbName);
5064	free(conn->replication);
5065	free(conn->pgservice);
5066	free(conn->pguser);
5067	if (conn->pgpass)
5068	{
5069		explicit_bzero(conn->pgpass, strlen(conn->pgpass));
5070		free(conn->pgpass);
5071	}
5072	free(conn->pgpassfile);
5073	free(conn->channel_binding);
5074	free(conn->keepalives);
5075	free(conn->keepalives_idle);
5076	free(conn->keepalives_interval);
5077	free(conn->keepalives_count);
5078	free(conn->sslmode);
5079	free(conn->sslnegotiation);
5080	free(conn->sslcompression);
5081	free(conn->sslkey);
5082	free(conn->sslcert);
5083	if (conn->sslpassword)
5084	{
5085		explicit_bzero(conn->sslpassword, strlen(conn->sslpassword));
5086		free(conn->sslpassword);
5087	}
5088	free(conn->sslcertmode);
5089	free(conn->sslrootcert);
5090	free(conn->sslcrl);
5091	free(conn->sslcrldir);
5092	free(conn->sslsni);
5093	free(conn->requirepeer);
5094	free(conn->gssencmode);
5095	free(conn->krbsrvname);
5096	free(conn->gsslib);
5097	free(conn->gssdelegation);
5098	free(conn->min_protocol_version);
5099	free(conn->max_protocol_version);
5100	free(conn->ssl_min_protocol_version);
5101	free(conn->ssl_max_protocol_version);
5102	free(conn->target_session_attrs);
5103	free(conn->require_auth);
5104	free(conn->load_balance_hosts);
5105	free(conn->scram_client_key);
5106	free(conn->scram_server_key);
5107	free(conn->sslkeylogfile);
5108	free(conn->oauth_issuer);
5109	free(conn->oauth_issuer_id);
5110	free(conn->oauth_discovery_uri);
5111	free(conn->oauth_client_id);
5112	free(conn->oauth_client_secret);
5113	free(conn->oauth_scope);
5114	/* Note that conn->Pfdebug is not ours to close or free */
5115	free(conn->events);
5116	pqReleaseConnHosts(conn);
5117	free(conn->connip);
5118	release_conn_addrinfo(conn);
5119	free(conn->scram_client_key_binary);
5120	free(conn->scram_server_key_binary);
5121	/* if this is a cancel connection, be_cancel_key may still be allocated */
5122	free(conn->be_cancel_key);
5123	free(conn->inBuffer);
5124	free(conn->outBuffer);
5125	free(conn->rowBuf);
5126	termPQExpBuffer(&conn->errorMessage);
5127	termPQExpBuffer(&conn->workBuffer);
5128
5129	free(conn);
5130}
5131
5132/*
5133 * pqReleaseConnHosts
5134 *	 - Free the host list in the PGconn.
5135 */
5136void
5137pqReleaseConnHosts(PGconn *conn)
5138{
5139	if (conn->connhost)
5140	{
5141		for (int i = 0; i < conn->nconnhost; ++i)
5142		{
5143			free(conn->connhost[i].host);
5144			free(conn->connhost[i].hostaddr);
5145			free(conn->connhost[i].port);
5146			if (conn->connhost[i].password != NULL)
5147			{
5148				explicit_bzero(conn->connhost[i].password,
5149							   strlen(conn->connhost[i].password));
5150				free(conn->connhost[i].password);
5151			}
5152		}
5153		free(conn->connhost);
5154		conn->connhost = NULL;
5155	}
5156}
5157
5158/*
5159 * store_conn_addrinfo
5160 *	 - copy addrinfo to PGconn object
5161 *
5162 * Copies the addrinfos from addrlist to the PGconn object such that the
5163 * addrinfos can be manipulated by libpq. Returns a positive integer on
5164 * failure, otherwise zero.
5165 */
5166static int
5167store_conn_addrinfo(PGconn *conn, struct addrinfo *addrlist)
5168{
5169	struct addrinfo *ai = addrlist;
5170
5171	conn->whichaddr = 0;
5172
5173	conn->naddr = 0;
5174	while (ai)
5175	{
5176		ai = ai->ai_next;
5177		conn->naddr++;
5178	}
5179
5180	conn->addr = calloc(conn->naddr, sizeof(AddrInfo));
5181	if (conn->addr == NULL)
5182	{
5183		libpq_append_conn_error(conn, "out of memory");
5184		return 1;
5185	}
5186
5187	ai = addrlist;
5188	for (int i = 0; i < conn->naddr; i++)
5189	{
5190		conn->addr[i].family = ai->ai_family;
5191
5192		memcpy(&conn->addr[i].addr.addr, ai->ai_addr,
5193			   ai->ai_addrlen);
5194		conn->addr[i].addr.salen = ai->ai_addrlen;
5195		ai = ai->ai_next;
5196	}
5197
5198	return 0;
5199}
5200
5201/*
5202 * release_conn_addrinfo
5203 *	 - Free any addrinfo list in the PGconn.
5204 */
5205static void
5206release_conn_addrinfo(PGconn *conn)
5207{
5208	if (conn->addr)
5209	{
5210		free(conn->addr);
5211		conn->addr = NULL;
5212	}
5213}
5214
5215/*
5216 * sendTerminateConn
5217 *	 - Send a terminate message to backend.
5218 */
5219static void
5220sendTerminateConn(PGconn *conn)
5221{
5222	/*
5223	 * The Postgres cancellation protocol does not have a notion of a
5224	 * Terminate message, so don't send one.
5225	 */
5226	if (conn->cancelRequest)
5227		return;
5228
5229	/*
5230	 * Note that the protocol doesn't allow us to send Terminate messages
5231	 * during the startup phase.
5232	 */
5233	if (conn->sock != PGINVALID_SOCKET && conn->status == CONNECTION_OK)
5234	{
5235		/*
5236		 * Try to send "close connection" message to backend. Ignore any
5237		 * error.
5238		 */
5239		pqPutMsgStart(PqMsg_Terminate, conn);
5240		pqPutMsgEnd(conn);
5241		(void) pqFlush(conn);
5242	}
5243}
5244
